raw_case:
Enteropathic SAPHO Syndrome in Ulcerative Colitis Responsive to Bisphosphonates
SAPHO syndrome, a rare inflammatory disorder of bone, joints, and skin, is named based on the presence of synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, and osteitis. The hallmark of SAPHO syndrome includes osteoarticular and dermatologic manifestations, however, rarer associations with inflammatory bowel disease (particularly Crohn's disease) have been documented. The literature on the relationship between SAPHO syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), especially ulcerative colitis (UC), remains limited. We report an unusual case of SAPHO syndrome in a patient with UC. Chest x-ray and MRI showed enlargement of the right first rib and adjacent sternum. Bone scintigraphy revealed hyperostosis and ankylosis of the costochondral junction, and bone biopsy revealed reactive bone and costal cartilage without findings of infection or malignancy. Complete resolution of symptoms was achieved 4 months after starting zoledronic acid without significant adverse events. The diagnosis of SAPHO syndrome in IBD patients is rare, even more so in UC patients, likely attributable to underdiagnosis given the clinical heterogeneity of SAPHO syndrome and overlap with the extra-intestinal manifestation of IBD. Our treatment approach provides critical data to the underreported literature on diagnosis and managing SAPHO syndrome in UC.

1. Introduction

SAPHO syndrome, a rare inflammatory disorder of bones, joints, and skin that was first described by Chamot in 1987 [1], is named after the presence of synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, and osteitis. SAPHO syndrome pathogenesis is not well understood, but it is commonly described as an autoinflammatory disorder with genetic, environmental, and immune components contributing to the disease process. SAPHO syndrome is a rare disease, with an estimated prevalence of 0.01%, and typically affects young-to-middle-aged adults (between 30 and 50 years with a female predominance); however, it can present at any age [2–4]. The hallmark of SAPHO syndrome includes osteoarticular and dermatologic manifestations; however, there are reports of a rare association with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), particularly Crohn's disease [5]. The literature on the relationship between SAPHO syndrome and IBD, especially ulcerative colitis (UC), and subsequent disease management remains limited, warranting further research to address such gaps. Here, we report a unique case of SAPHO syndrome in a patient with UC that was successfully treated with local steroid injections and zoledronic acid.

2. Case Summary

A 60-year-old male was referred to our Rheumatology department for further evaluation and management of an unknown autoimmune disorder. He had a 7-year medical history of UC (diagnosed in 2016) treated with various biologic agents, including infliximab (discontinued in June 2019 after 16–18 months due to the development of pustular psoriasis in March 2019), vedolizumab (August 2019 through March 2020; discontinued due to developing pulmonary emboli), and currently ustekinumab (well-controlled on 90 mg every 8 weeks; ongoing since August 2019). He initially presented with a several-year history of persistent right-sided chest pain (beginning May 2019) that was exacerbated by movement and radiated to the right arm and upper back. He had previously trialed NSAIDs with minor relief, and oral glucocorticoids for many months with moderate symptom relief, however, steroid discontinuation caused a flare of his chest pain. He had also previously received a local glucocorticoid injection with adequate relief for several months before presenting to our clinic. He denied any history of inflammatory polyarthritis, warm or swollen joints, constitutional symptoms, or new dermatologic lesions (his previous pustular psoriasis resolved after infliximab discontinuation). Physical examination revealed tenderness of the medial aspect of the right clavicle. Routine laboratory studies revealed elevated inflammatory markers (erythrocyte sedimentation rate [ESR] 92 mm/hr, C-reactive protein [CRP] 25 mg/L), while complete blood counts, an autoimmune panel (including Scl-70, rheumatoid factor, double-stranded DNA, anti-SSA/SSB, c-ANCA, p-ANCA, ANA, RNP, RNA polymerase III, and Jo1) and liver function tests were within normal limits. HLA-B27 testing was negative. Radiographic assessment, including a chest X-ray (Figure 1) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Figure 2), showed enlargement of the right first rib and adjacent sternum. Whole-body bone scintigraphy (Figure 3) showed an expansile lesion in the distal right first rib with associated hyperostosis and ankylosis of the costochondral junction, without any other axial and/or appendicular skeleton findings. A bone biopsy (Figure 4) of the right anterior first rib revealed reactive bone and costal cartilage without findings of infection or malignancy. Given the absence of other competing etiologies, a diagnosis of SAPHO syndrome was made (in 2023). He was started on treatment with sulfasalazine (2 g daily) with local glucocorticoid injections (every 4–6 weeks). Notably, other anti-TNFs were deferred given his prior history of infliximab-induced pustular psoriasis, and methotrexate was deferred given his reluctance to reduce frequent social drinking. This approach provided moderate symptom relief; however, he experienced significant bloating while on sulfasalazine, prompting its discontinuation 1 month later. He was subsequently started on zoledronic acid (1g) 4 months later with complete symptom resolution during a 4-month follow-up.

3. Discussion

The present study reports a unique case of osteoarticular SAPHO syndrome in a patient with ulcerative colitis treated with local steroid injections and bisphosphonates. The patient tolerated treatment well without significant adverse events and exhibited a great clinical response at 9 months of follow-up.SAPHO syndrome exhibits clinical heterogeneity and lacks validated diagnostic criteria, which poses significant diagnostic challenges for clinicians. SAPHO syndrome is often a diagnosis of exclusion, requiring a thorough history, physical examination, and radiographic/laboratory assessments to rule out alternative etiologies, such as malignancy, osteomyelitis, and seronegative spondyloarthropathies. Osteoarticular manifestations, most notably hyperostosis and/or osteitis, are a hallmark of SAPHO syndrome, irrespective of active dermatologic findings [2, 6]. Musculoskeletal changes are typically identified on plain radiographs and advanced imaging (e.g., CT, MRI, and bone scintigraphy) [7, 8]. Laboratory findings often include elevations in nonspecific inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP. Our patient's current presentation seemingly lacked cutaneous manifestations of SAPHO syndrome (e.g., acneiform [commonly nodulocystic acne] and neutrophilic [commonly palmoplantar pustulosis] dermatoses), but the presence of isolated osteoarticular involvement (right sternoclavicular joint hyperostosis) in the context of ulcerative colitis and absence of alternative etiologies (no evidence of malignancy or osteomyelitis on bone biopsy; no evidence of sacroiliac joint involvement on imaging and HLA-B27 negative), made SAPHO syndrome the most likely diagnosis [9]. Our patient had a recent history of pustular psoriasis attributable to infliximab (supported by symptom resolution upon infliximab discontinuation); however, the pustular skin lesions of SAPHO syndrome and pustular psoriasis are clinically indistinguishable, making it difficult to completely rule out dermatologic manifestations of SAPHO syndrome in the present case. Notably, skin lesions are absent in approximately one-third of SAPHO syndrome patients, and most dermatologic manifestations occur within 2 years of diagnosis, suggesting that there are unknown, underlying factors predisposing to dermatologic manifestations in SAPHO syndrome [10].There are no standardized treatment algorithms for SAPHO syndrome due to its rarity and consequent paucity of available literature; current treatment approaches are based on observational data. Osteoarticular manifestations (without dermatologic components) are initially treated with NSAIDs, followed by short courses of oral glucocorticoids [11]. Steroid-sparing immunosuppressive agents (e.g., methotrexate and tumor necrosis factor [TNF]-alpha inhibition) are used in refractory cases (either due to inadequate response or intolerance of initial agents). For patients with cutaneous manifestations, oral tetracyclines and oral retinoids may be beneficial [12]. IL-17 and IL-12/23 inhibition, sulfasalazine, Janus kinase (JAK) inhibition, and bisphosphonates, have reported efficacy in treating refractory cases of osteoarticular disease [13–16]. Relatedly, local intra-articular glucocorticoids have been observed to provide significant symptom relief for SAPHO syndrome patients with sternocostoclavicular joint involvement (which was also true for our patient with right sternoclavicular involvement) [17]. Our patient had minimal relief with NSAIDs, moderate relief from intermittent glucocorticoid injections and oral corticosteroids, and a positive response to escalation of care with sulfasalazine (which was discontinued due to intolerance) and zoledronic acid. This supports the use of disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) and bisphosphonates in treating SAPHO syndrome in patients with ulcerative colitis. Of note, our patient was concurrently taking ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23) for his ulcerative colitis, which has been reported to be effective in SAPHO syndrome [13]. However, he had been on ustekinumab for several months at the time of his presentation to our clinic (while actively symptomatic), suggesting that it was not controlling his osteoarticular symptoms as monotherapy, but might have had a synergistic or augmentative effect when combined with bisphosphonates.SAPHO syndrome has been reported to have rarer disease manifestations, such as IBD. Both SAPHO and IBD are chronic, inflammatory conditions that can affect multiple organs. The relationship between concurrent SAPHO syndrome and IBD, particularly in the practical context of disease management, is understudied and requires further research. Notably, SAPHO syndrome associated with IBD has a reported female predominance and occurs most frequently in patients with Crohn's disease, with prevalence rates varying from less than 1%–10% [2, 5, 6]. A systematic literature review found that 4.8% of SAPHO syndrome patients also had IBD, whereas 0.2% of IBD patients had SAPHO syndrome, suggesting that a diagnosis of SAPHO syndrome confers an increased likelihood for a concurrent IBD diagnosis (as opposed to the reverse) [5]. A separate study identified 15 patients with both SAPHO syndrome and IBD, similarly reporting a predominance of female patients and Crohn's disease [18]. Interestingly, they reported that IBD was diagnosed before SAPHO syndrome in the majority of cases (14/15; 93.3%), often with a time interval of at least 3 years between diagnoses (10/15; 66.7%) [18]. The literature specifically addressing SAPHO syndrome in ulcerative colitis patients remains limited, consisting predominantly of case reports. One case involving a 39-year-old with UC observed a significant clinical response to pamidronate [19], while another case involving a 54-year-old man with UC documented a positive response to tofacitinib (a JAK inhibitor) [14], further suggesting the utility of bisphosphonates and biologics in treating SAPHO syndrome. Ultimately, the diagnosis of SAPHO syndrome in IBD patients is both rare and challenging, with several studies reporting a prevalence of less than 1% [5]. Underdiagnosis may contribute to this rarity, as the clinical features of SAPHO syndrome can mimic the extra-intestinal manifestations of IBD (e.g., neutrophilic dermatoses). Thus, the present study adds crucial clinical context and critical data to the underreported literature on enteropathic SAPHO syndrome in UC with subsequent management approaches.We report an unusual case of SAPHO syndrome in a patient with ulcerative colitis. The diagnosis of SAPHO syndrome in IBD patients is rare, even more so in ulcerative colitis patients, as most studies have documented a Crohn's disease-predominant IBD association. Underdiagnosis may contribute to this rarity, as SAPHO syndrome displays heterogeneously complex clinical manifestations that can resemble other dermatologic and rheumatic diseases, including the extra-intestinal manifestations of IBD. Ultimately, further investigation of enteropathy SAPHO syndrome variants is warranted to determine the extent of association with IBD (and particularly UC) and to facilitate early recognition of concurrent disease and optimal management and improved outcomes in affected individuals.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 60-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Persistent right-sided chest pain radiating to the right arm and upper back, exacerbated by movement.
- **History of Present Illness:** Seven-year history of ulcerative colitis (UC), treated with various biologic agents, including infliximab (discontinued due to pustular psoriasis), vedolizumab (discontinued due to pulmonary emboli), and currently ustekinumab for UC (ongoing). Symptomatic chest pain since May 2019, minimally relieved by NSAIDs and moderate improvement with oral and local glucocorticoids.
- **Past Medical History:** Ulcerative colitis; history of infliximab-induced pustular psoriasis.
- **Personal History:** Refusal to reduce frequent alcohol consumption.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Tenderness at the medial aspect of the right clavicle. No evidence of inflammatory polyarthritis, swollen joints, or active dermatologic lesions. Prior pustular psoriasis resolved post-infliximab discontinuation.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Routine labs: Elevated ESR (92 mm/hr) and CRP (25 mg/L); other autoimmune markers and HLA-B27 negative.
  - Imaging: Chest X-ray and MRI revealed enlargement of the right first rib and adjacent sternum. Bone scintigraphy showed hyperostosis and ankylosis at the right costochondral junction, with no other skeletal findings.
  - Bone biopsy: Reactive bone and costal cartilage without evidence of malignancy or infection.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - Persistent osteoarticular symptoms, history of UC, elevated inflammatory markers, and imaging findings prompted consideration of SAPHO syndrome, extra-intestinal manifestations of UC (e.g., seronegative spondyloarthropathies), osteomyelitis, and malignancy.

2. **Exclusion of Non-SAPHO Diagnoses:**
   - **Malignancy and infection:** Excluded by bone biopsy findings, which revealed reactive bone and cartilage without evidence of tumor or infection.
   - **Seronegative spondyloarthropathy:** Sacroiliac joint involvement was absent, and HLA-B27 was negative—less likely diagnosis.
   - **UC-associated extra-intestinal manifestation:** While this overlaps with SAPHO syndrome, radiographic findings of hyperostosis and ankylosis and absence of dermatologic findings at presentation narrowed the diagnosis.

3. **Confirmation of SAPHO Syndrome:**
   - Diagnosis was supported by osteoarticular findings (hyperostosis and ankylosis of the costochondral junction), UC history, and exclusion of competing etiologies. The lack of active cutaneous manifestations is consistent, as one-third of SAPHO patients may lack skin involvement.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: SAPHO syndrome in a patient with ulcerative colitis.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

Step 1: Analyze the Patient's Chief Complaint and History

The patient is a 60-year-old male presenting with persistent right-sided chest pain radiating to the right arm and upper back, exacerbated by movement. This pain has been present since May 2019 and has not been fully relieved by NSAIDs or glucocorticoids. Given the duration and persistence of the symptoms, it's crucial to consider both musculoskeletal and systemic causes.

Step 2: Consider the Context of Ulcerative Colitis Treatment

The patient has a seven-year history of ulcerative colitis, managed with several biologic agents. He was on infliximab but discontinued due to pustular psoriasis, then switched to vedolizumab but discontinued due to pulmonary emboli, and is currently on ustekinumab. The history of biologic therapy raises the possibility of drug-induced adverse effects or immune-related disorders.

Step 3: Evaluate the Patient's Personal and Family History

The patient refuses to reduce his frequent alcohol consumption, which could contribute to liver disease or other conditions affecting pain perception or metabolic processes. No family history is reported, which reduces the likelihood of hereditary conditions but doesn't eliminate the need to consider genetic factors in certain diagnoses.

Step 4: Review the Physical Examination Findings

Physical examination reveals tenderness at the medial aspect of the right clavicle, with no evidence of inflammatory polyarthritis, swollen joints, or active dermatologic lesions. The resolution of prior pustular psoriasis post-infliximab discontinuation suggests a drug-related etiology for that condition.

Step 5: Interpret Laboratory Test Results

Routine labs show elevated ESR (92 mm/hr) and CRP (25 mg/L), indicating ongoing inflammation. Autoimmune markers and HLA-B27 are negative, which reduces the probability of certain inflammatory arthritides but does not rule them out entirely.

Step 6: Assess Imaging Studies

Chest X-ray and MRI show enlargement of the right first rib and adjacent sternum. Bone scintigraphy reveals hyperostosis and ankylosis at the right costochondral junction, with no other skeletal abnormalities. These findings suggest localized bone and cartilage changes, possibly due to inflammation or other pathological processes.

Step 7: Consider Bone Biopsy Results

The bone biopsy shows reactive bone and costal cartilage without evidence of malignancy or infection. This indicates that the bone changes are a reaction to some stimulus, potentially inflammatory, but not neoplastic or infectious.

Step 8: Synthesize Information to Formulate a Differential Diagnosis

Given the persistent chest pain, history of ulcerative colitis with biologic therapy, and imaging findings of hyperostosis and ankylosis at the costochondral junction, several conditions should be considered:

- Rheumatoid arthritis or other inflammatory arthritides, although autoimmune markers are negative.

- Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH), which can cause hyperostosis and ankylosis, typically in the spine but can involve other areas.

- Tumors or malignancies, though the biopsy did not show malignancy.

- Infections, which are unlikely given the negative biopsy and absence of infection markers.

- Calcifying tendinitis or other localized inflammatory conditions affecting the chest wall.

Step 9: Consider the Role of Biologic Therapy in Symptom Onset

The onset of chest pain coincides with the period of biologic therapy for ulcerative colitis. Although the specific agent in use at the time of symptom onset isn't explicitly stated, the history of adverse reactions to previous biologics (psoriasis with infliximab, pulmonary emboli with vedolizumab) suggests a possible drug-related etiology.

Step 10: Evaluate the Effect of Treatments on Symptoms

The patient has tried NSAIDs and glucocorticoids, with only partial relief. This suggests that the underlying cause may not be fully responsive to anti-inflammatory treatments, or that there is a structural component to the pain.

Step 11: Consider the Patient's Refusal to Reduce Alcohol Consumption

Persistent alcohol use could potentially contribute to liver disease or other conditions that might secondarily cause musculoskeletal pain, although no specific liver function tests are mentioned.

Step 12: Review the Specific Imaging Findings

The enlargement of the right first rib and adjacent sternum, along with hyperostosis and ankylosis at the costochondral junction, points towards a process affecting the bones and cartilage in that region. The fact that these changes are localized suggests a focal inflammatory or degenerative process.

Step 13: Consider the Possibility of Chest Wall Pain Syndromes

Chest wall pain syndromes, such as Tietze's syndrome or costochondritis, can present with chest pain and localized tenderness. However, Tietze's syndrome typically involves swelling of the costal cartilages, and costochondritis often presents with tenderness at the costosternal junctions. The imaging findings in this case suggest more significant structural changes.

Step 14: Assess for Possibility of Reactive Arthritis or Other Spondyloarthropathies

尽管HLA-B27阴性，但不能完全排除脊柱关节病的可能性，因为并非所有病例都与HLA-B27相关。然而，患者的症状和检查结果并不典型。

Step 15: Consider the Possibility of a Rare Condition Affecting the Chest Wall

Given the atypical presentation and the presence of hyperostosis and ankylosis, a rare condition such as synovial osteochondromatosis or fibroblastoma fascia could be considered, although these are uncommon.

Step 16: Evaluate the Biopsy Results in Context

The bone biopsy showing reactive bone and costal cartilage without evidence of malignancy or infection suggests that the changes are due to a non-specific inflammatory or reparative process. This supports a diagnosis of a localized inflammatory condition rather than a neoplastic or infectious etiology.

Step 17: Consider the Role of Chronic Inflammation from Ulcerative Colitis

Given the patient's history of ulcerative colitis, it's possible that chronic inflammation could lead to extraintestinal manifestations affecting the musculoskeletal system. However, the specific presentation and imaging findings are not typical of known extraintestinal manifestations of UC.

Step 18: Review the Treatment History and Potential Adverse Effects

The patient has had adverse reactions to previous biologic therapies, including pustular psoriasis with infliximab and pulmonary emboli with vedolizumab. It's possible that the current chest pain could be another adverse effect related to biologic therapy, although ustekinumab is currently being used without reported issues.

Step 19: Consider the Need for Further Diagnostic Workup

Given the persistence of symptoms and the atypical nature of the imaging findings, further evaluation may be warranted. This could include additional imaging studies, such as a CT scan or PET scan, or consultation with a rheumatologist or orthopedic specialist.

Step 20: Formulate a Provisional Diagnosis Based on Available Data

Considering all the above points, a provisional diagnosis of diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) affecting the right first rib and sternum seems plausible. DISH is characterized by hyperostosis along the anterior aspect of the spine and can involve other areas, leading to ankylosis and pain. However, this remains a consideration, and further evaluation is needed to confirm.

### Answer:

Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis (DISH) limited to the right first rib and sternum, causing localized chest pain and hyperostosis.

The patient is a 60-year-old male presenting with persistent right-sided chest pain radiating to the right arm and upper back, exacerbated by movement. This pain has been present since May 2019 and has not been fully relieved by NSAIDs or glucocorticoids. Given the duration and persistence of the symptoms, it's crucial to consider both musculoskeletal and systemic causes.
Step 2: Consider the Context of Ulcerative Colitis Treatment

The patient has a seven-year history of ulcerative colitis, managed with several biologic agents. He was on infliximab but discontinued due to pustular psoriasis, then switched to vedolizumab but discontinued due to pulmonary emboli, and is currently on ustekinumab. The history of biologic therapy raises the possibility of drug-induced adverse effects or immune-related disorders.
Step 3: Evaluate the Patient's Personal and Family History

The patient refuses to reduce his frequent alcohol consumption, which could contribute to liver disease or other conditions affecting pain perception or metabolic processes. No family history is reported, which reduces the likelihood of hereditary conditions but doesn't eliminate the need to consider genetic factors in certain diagnoses.
Step 4: Review the Physical Examination Findings

Physical examination reveals tenderness at the medial aspect of the right clavicle, with no evidence of inflammatory polyarthritis, swollen joints, or active dermatologic lesions. The resolution of prior pustular psoriasis post-infliximab discontinuation suggests a drug-related etiology for that condition.

Routine labs show elevated ESR (92 mm/hr) and CRP (25 mg/L), indicating ongoing inflammation. Autoimmune markers and HLA-B27 are negative, which reduces the probability of certain inflammatory arthritides but does not rule them out entirely.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial considerations included persistent osteoarticular symptoms, history of UC, elevated inflammatory markers, and imaging findings, prompting evaluation for SAPHO syndrome, extra-intestinal manifestations of UC, osteomyelitis, and malignancy.
Malignancy and infection were excluded by bone biopsy findings showing reactive bone and cartilage without evidence of tumor or infection.
Seronegative spondyloarthropathy was less likely due to absent sacroiliac joint involvement and negative HLA-B27 test.
UC-associated extra-intestinal manifestation was considered but ruled out by radiographic findings of hyperostosis and ankylosis and absence of dermatologic findings at presentation.
Diagnosis was confirmed by osteoarticular findings (hyperostosis and ankylosis of the costochondral junction), UC history, and exclusion of competing etiologies.
Final diagnosis: SAPHO syndrome in a patient with ulcerative colitis.
